our_argument_summary: Our review makes two GPS-related arguments across two locations: (1) In the HTML Part 4 'GPS Accuracy and Relativity' section, we argue that GPS requires relativistic corrections (+38.3 μs/day net) and without them position error accumulates at ~10 km/day, which the dome model cannot account for since it rejects relativity. We also address the anticipated WGS84-is-just-software objection. (2) In the DOCX Section 4.3 'GPS Satellites Above the Dome', we argue that GPS satellites orbit at 20,200 km — above the dome's maximum height of 8,537 km — so how do signals reach the ground? We then explain that GPS requires Keplerian orbits and relativistic corrections tied to spherical-Earth geometry.

relevant_files: No monitor.py or pull_data.py found in raw-text/ for dome repository
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: The dome site does not appear to have any automated monitoring or validation of GPS-related claims. GPS Sagnac appears only as domain #19 in the 20-domain dashboard on the site.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: N/A — no GPS validation exists in the dome's automation
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The dome site does not directly address GPS relativistic corrections. The WGS84 objection (GPS works because of math, not physics) is the primary escape route used by flat-earth communities generally, not specifically by the dome model's author.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None found — our review does not make specific claims about how the dome's code handles GPS.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: No monitor.py or pull_data.py files found in raw-text/. No dome automation for GPS domain exists. GPS appears only in the 20-domain dashboard prose.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: GPS is not relabeled by the dome model — it is largely ignored. The dome site lists GPS Sagnac as a domain but provides no dome-specific explanation of how GPS works under dome geometry.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: N/A — the dome model does not claim to predict GPS accuracy. It simply lists it as a domain where it asserts the Sagnac correction proves absolute simultaneity.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No derivation from dome geometry for GPS behavior. The dome site mentions Sagnac correction but does not derive GPS accuracy or relativistic corrections from dome parameters.

holes_found.description: The HTML and DOCX present different framings that aren't clearly connected. The HTML section 'GPS Accuracy and Relativity' focuses on relativistic clock corrections and anticipated WGS84 objection. The DOCX section 4.3 'GPS Satellites Above the Dome' leads with the physical altitude argument (satellites above the dome). A dome defender could argue we're scatter-shotting multiple angles without committing to the strongest one. The altitude argument is actually MORE devastating (physically impossible, no hand-waving possible) but gets less real estate in the HTML version.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider adding the altitude argument to the HTML version. 'GPS satellites orbit at 20,200 km — above the dome's claimed maximum height of 8,537 km' is a devastating geometric constraint that doesn't require the reader to understand relativity.

holes_found.description: The +38.3 μs/day figure needs clearer sourcing. Our text says '(1) atomic clocks in orbit run faster than ground clocks by 45.9 microseconds per day due to weaker gravity (general relativistic effect), and (2) clocks run faster due to orbital motion at 14 km/s (special relativistic effect), partly offsetting to a net +38.3 μs/day.' The second effect should say clocks run SLOWER due to orbital motion (SR time dilation slows moving clocks). The net is 45.9 - 7.2 ≈ 38.7 μs/day, which rounds to 38.6 or 38.7 depending on the source. The commonly cited figure is +38 μs/day. Our phrasing says 'partly offsetting to a net +38.3 μs/day' — the offset calculation (45.9 - 7.6 = 38.3) works only with a specific SR value. More importantly, the text says 'clocks run faster due to orbital motion' — this is WRONG. SR causes moving clocks to run SLOWER, which is the partial offset.
holes_found.recommendation: Fix the direction of the SR effect: 'clocks run SLOWER due to orbital motion at ~3.9 km/s relative to the geoid' (not 14 km/s — that's the orbital speed, not the relevant velocity for the SR calculation which accounts for the ground's rotational velocity). Or more precisely: GR makes satellite clocks run faster by ~45 μs/day; SR makes them run slower by ~7 μs/day; net is ~+38 μs/day faster, requiring the clocks to be pre-adjusted. The 14 km/s figure is close to the orbital velocity but the SR calculation is done relative to the ECI frame.

holes_found.description: The 'dome model predicts GPS drifts; every practical test falsifies it' claim is slightly strawmanning. The dome model doesn't explicitly predict GPS drift — it simply doesn't address GPS mechanics at all, which is arguably worse. The dome model's Open Problems list (OPEN-001, OPEN-006) concedes reliance on WGS84 coordinates. A careful dome defender could say 'we don't claim GPS doesn't work — we just say the math works because it's a coordinate transform, not because the underlying geometry is spherical.' We address this objection in the next paragraph, but the initial claim overstates what the dome model actually says.
holes_found.recommendation: Rephrase from 'The dome model predicts GPS drifts' to something like 'A dome geometry would require different relativistic corrections than those used — the dome model cannot explain why corrections calibrated for a spherical Earth produce accurate positioning.' This is both more precise and harder to rebut.

holes_found.description: The section mentions the dome model's 'gravitational and EM coupling constant κ = 1.67 nT/μGal; no relativistic corrections necessary' — but this κ value is about magnetic-gravity coupling (WIN-012), not about GPS or relativity. The connection between κ and GPS is unclear. A dome defender could ask 'what does our EM-gravity coupling constant have to do with GPS clocks?'
holes_found.recommendation: Replace the κ reference with the dome model's actual position on GPS, which is that it claims Sagnac correction proves absolute simultaneity and that WGS84 is a mathematical convenience. Or drop the 'dome model' framing and go straight to 'The test.'

holes_found.description: The anticipated objection paragraph is strong but could be strengthened. The WGS84-is-just-software objection is the standard flat-earth response to GPS. Our rebuttal focuses on clock drift rates. But there's an even more direct counter: GPS satellite visibility patterns. At any point on a spherical Earth, you see 6-12 satellites above the horizon. On a flat disc, you would see ALL 24+ satellites simultaneously from every location (unless the dome blocks them — but then signals can't reach you). The visibility geometry alone falsifies flat geometry, without needing to understand relativity.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence about satellite visibility geometry: 'Furthermore, the pattern of which satellites are visible from a given location at a given time matches spherical-Earth orbital mechanics to arcsecond precision; a flat-disc geometry would produce entirely different visibility patterns.'

stronger_arguments.description: The altitude argument deserves prominence: GPS satellites orbit at 20,200 km, which is above the dome's claimed maximum firmament height of 8,537 km (at the pole) and well above ~2,100 km at the equatorial rim. Either GPS satellites don't exist (contradicted by amateur radio operators who track and communicate with them), or they exist above the dome (which means there is no dome), or the dome is permeable (which undermines the resonant-cavity model that the dome site depends on for Schumann resonance claims).

The dome model's own OPEN-001 ('Dome-native coordinates without WGS84 — IN PROGRESS') is a devastating self-admission. The model cannot navigate without WGS84 — a coordinate system built on a spherical oblate Earth. If dome geometry were correct, WGS84 coordinates would not produce accurate distances, yet the dome model explicitly relies on WGS84 coordinates for all its distance calculations. This is mentioned elsewhere in our review but should be cross-referenced here as it directly supports the GPS argument.

Two-Line Element (TLE) sets: Amateur astronomers, satellite trackers, and ham radio operators independently verify satellite positions using TLE orbital elements, which are computed assuming Keplerian orbits around a sphere. If Earth's geometry were different, these predictions would fail — but they don't. Anyone with a $30 SDR dongle can decode GPS L1 signals and verify the satellite's orbital elements directly. This is a citizen-science-accessible test.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model's implicit challenge has a grain of legitimacy in one narrow sense: the relationship between coordinate systems and physical geometry IS subtle. WGS84 is indeed a mathematical model, and mathematical models can produce correct predictions from incorrect physical assumptions within their domain of validity (e.g., epicycles 'worked' for centuries). The dome author is not wrong that coordinate math can abstract away underlying geometry for some calculations.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The kernel of truth fails because GPS doesn't just use coordinates — it uses physical clocks whose tick rates depend on the actual gravitational field and actual velocity (both measured, not assumed). The relativistic corrections are derived from, and confirmed by, the physical geometry. If the physical geometry were a flat disc, the gravitational potential at satellite altitude would be different, the velocity time dilation would be different, and the corrections would fail. Additionally, GPS satellite visibility patterns, signal propagation delays, and orbital predictions all depend on physical geometry, not just coordinate convenience. WGS84 works for GPS because it accurately describes physical reality, not because math is geometry-agnostic.

deeper_analysis:
This kill-shot is structurally sound but has a surprising physics error in the SR direction and could be made significantly stronger.

The core GPS argument has three independent legs:
1. PHYSICAL ALTITUDE: Satellites orbit at 20,200 km, above the dome's 8,537 km maximum. This is geometric and unarguable.
2. RELATIVISTIC CORRECTIONS: The +38 μs/day clock correction is a physical measurement of spacetime curvature, not a software convention.
3. ORBITAL MECHANICS: Satellite positions are predicted by Keplerian orbits around a sphere, verified to meter-level precision by billions of users daily.

All three legs are individually devastating. Our HTML section focuses almost exclusively on leg 2, while the DOCX covers all three. The HTML is weaker than it needs to be.

The SR direction error (claiming orbital motion makes clocks run FASTER when it actually makes them run SLOWER) is the kind of mistake a dome defender would seize on. Getting basic relativity wrong in a section arguing that relativity confirms the globe would be embarrassing. The GR effect makes satellite clocks tick faster (weaker gravity = less time dilation); the SR effect makes them tick slower (higher velocity = more time dilation). The net is positive (clocks run fast) because the GR effect dominates. Our text says '(2) clocks run faster due to orbital motion' — this reverses the SR effect.

The anticipated objection paragraph about WGS84 is good but could be more concrete. The strongest response to 'WGS84 is just math' is: 'Then predict where GPS satellite L1-C/A signals will arrive, at what time, from which direction, using dome geometry. If your geometry is correct, your predictions will work. If they don't, your geometry is wrong.' Nobody has done this because flat-disc signal propagation predictions would be wildly wrong.

One consideration from the other side: A sophisticated dome defender might argue that 'GPS doesn't actually use Keplerian orbits — it uses ephemeris data uploaded from ground stations, and the ground stations could be computing using any model as long as the ephemeris predictions match reality.' This is technically true in a narrow sense — GPS satellites broadcast ephemeris data that receivers use, not raw Keplerian elements. But the ephemeris data IS computed from orbital mechanics models that assume a spherical Earth. The ICD-GPS-200 specification is publicly available and explicitly uses WGS84 geodetic coordinates and Keplerian orbital elements. You can verify this yourself.

citations_verified: GPS.gov (https://www.gps.gov) — valid, official US government GPS information site
Spot the Station (https://spotthestation.nasa.gov) — valid, NASA ISS tracking tool (referenced in DOCX)
citations_unchecked: The +38.3 μs/day figure — commonly cited in physics education but should reference Ashby (2003) Living Reviews in Relativity or the ICD-GPS-200 specification for authoritative sourcing
The 45.9 μs/day GR effect and ~7 μs/day SR effect — standard values but no primary source cited in our text
The ~10 km/day drift claim — this is the widely cited value from popular physics sources, derived from 38 μs/day × speed of light, but should ideally cite Ashby (2003) or a GPS engineering reference
